summary:
The Yangban Class (c. 1392–1910 AD) was the Confucian scholarly-official aristocracy that dominated the Joseon dynasty of Korea — a hereditary elite defined by mastery of Chinese classical learning, success in the civil service examinations (the gwageo system), and the social prestige that derived from office-holding. Unlike the Chinese system on which it was modeled, Korean Yangban status was hereditary as well as meritocratic: a family that had produced a successful examination candidate retained Yangban status for generations, creating a self-perpetuating aristocracy that combined lineage prestige with educational aspiration. By the 18th century, Yangban families constituted perhaps 10-15% of the population but monopolized the examinations, government positions, and land ownership.

The Yangban class left an indelible mark on Korean culture — their emphasis on Confucian learning, classical Chinese literacy, and filial piety shaped Korean social values, educational culture, and gender relations for centuries. The Neo-Confucian orthodoxy that the Yangban embodied encouraged social conservatism, particularly regarding women (the system of strict gender segregation in elite families was a Yangban innovation), and factional political conflict (the intense factional strife of 16th-17th century Joseon politics was largely a Yangban phenomenon). However, the Yangban educational tradition also produced Korean scientific, literary, and artistic achievements: the invention of Hangul script by [[event:3294|Sejong the Great]] was made possible by royal scholars who were themselves part of this learned culture. The Yangban system, undermined by the devastation of the [[event:2420|Imjin War]] and 19th century social change, was formally abolished in 1894.
